coredata.dc:title: Hyperbranched polyurethane/reduced carbon dot-zinc oxide nanocomposite-mediated solar-assisted photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminant: An approach towards environmental remediation
coredata.dc:creator: Duarah, Rituparna
Karak, Niranjan
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis has carved out a niche in the recent times, as an environmentally benign, renewable and innovative technology for degradation of common organic pollutants in nature. The present work focuses on the development of a sustainable hyperbranched polyurethane (HPU) nanocomposite with reduced carbon dot-zinc oxide nanohybrid (RCD-ZnO) as a prospective solar energy-assisted heterogeneous photocatalyst for degradation of anionic surfactant contaminants. RCD-ZnO nanohybrid was synthesized by a simple and eco-friendly protocol by employing Colocasia esculenta leaf extract as bio-reductant, and fabricated with a starch-based HPU by an in situ polymerization process. Structural characterizations were performed by FT-IR, UV–Visible, EDX, TEM, TGA and DSC techniques. Optical studies revealed optical band gap of RCD-ZnO (2.12 eV) was reduced considerably in comparison to bare RCD (3.18 eV) and ZnO (3.3 eV). The RCD-ZnO nanohybrid-reinforced HPU nanocomposite displayed significant enhancement in mechanical properties like strength (1.9 folds), elongation (1.39 folds), scratch hardness (1.53 folds), toughness (3.54 folds) over pristine HPU as well as improved thermal stability (25 °C enhancement). Furthermore, the RCD-ZnO nanohybrid-reinforced HPU nanocomposite demonstrated high efficiency as a heterogeneous recyclable photocatalyst for degradation of aqueous surfactant contaminants, dodecylbenzenesulfonate (96.7% in 110 min) and commercial detergent (94.8% in 150 min) upon exposure to sunlight, following the pseudo-first order kinetics model. Thus, the current work holds good potential for the development of such high performance sustainable nanocomposite as an efficient solar energy-assisted heterogeneous recyclable photocatalyst for large scale applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Starch
Hyperbranched polyurethane nanocomposite
Solar-assisted photocatalyst
Organic pollutant degradation
Recyclable catalyst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Colocasia esculenta aqueous phytoextract
Preparation and characterization of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid
Fabrication and characterization of HPU/RZN nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of (i) CD, (ii) RCD-ZnO; (b) XRD pattern of RCD-ZnO; (c) Raman spectra of (i) CD and (ii) RCD-ZnO; and EDX maps of (d) ZnO and (e) RCD within in RCD-ZnO nanohybrid.

TEM images of RCD (red lines) and ZnO (white lines) at magnification (a) 5 nm, (b) 2 nm; (c) SAED pattern of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid; IIFT images showing the lattice fringes of (d) ZnO and (e) RCD in the nanohybrid. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) FTIR spectra of (i) HPU, (ii) HPU/RZN0.5, (iii) HPU/RZN1, (iv) HPU/RZN2; (b) XRD patterns of (i) HPU, (ii) HPU/RZN0.5, (iii) HPU/RZN1, (iv) HPU/RZN2; TEM image of HPU/RZN2 at magnification (c) 0.5 µm, (d) 2 nm with lattice fringes of RCD (red lines) and ZnO (white lines) of nanohybrid at inset; and (e) SAED pattern of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid in the HPU matrix. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Stress–strain profiles, (b) TGA thermograms, (c) DTG curves and (d) DSC curves of (i) HPU, (ii) HPU/RZN0.5, (iii) HPU/RZN1 and (iv) HPU/RZN2.
UV–visible spectra of (a) CD, C. esculenta leaf extract and ZnCl2 mixture, (b) RCD-ZnO; and (αhν)1/2 vs hν plots of (c) RCD, (d) RCD-ZnO.
(a) PL spectra of RCD and RCD-ZnO at concentration of 1 mg/mL at 340 nm excitation and (b) PL spectra of RCD-ZnO with variation of excitation wavelength (320–420) nm.

Plots of UV absorbance against wavelength at different times for the degradation of (a) DBS and (b) commercial detergent in the presence of sunlight; (c) degradation curves and (d) fitting degradation curves of DBS and detergent under given conditions; and (e) photocatalytic degradation of DBS using recycled HPU/RZN2 five successive times.
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism by HPU nanocomposite.
Mechanical properties of HPU and its nanocomposites.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Calculated by integrating the area under stress–strain curves.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermal properties of HPU and its nanocomposites.
Photocatalytic degradation of surfactants using different photocatalysts.

row.entry: TiO2 nanoparticle (5 mg) along with 1 mM K2S2O8, pH = 6

head.ce:title: Hyperbranched polyurethane/reduced carbon dot-zinc oxide nanocomposite-mediated solar-assisted photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminant: An approach towards environmental remediation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Polymer and Nanomaterial Laboratory, Department of Chemical Sciences, Tezpur University, Napaam, Tezpur, Assam 784028, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Starch-modified hyperbranched polyurethane/reduced carbon dot-zinc oxide nanocomposite as robust and recyclable solar-assisted photocatalyst for degradation of organic contaminants.

Photocatalysis has carved out a niche in the recent times, as an environmentally benign, renewable and innovative technology for degradation of common organic pollutants in nature. The present work focuses on the development of a sustainable hyperbranched polyurethane (HPU) nanocomposite with reduced carbon dot-zinc oxide nanohybrid (RCD-ZnO) as a prospective solar energy-assisted heterogeneous photocatalyst for degradation of anionic surfactant contaminants. RCD-ZnO nanohybrid was synthesized by a simple and eco-friendly protocol by employing Colocasia esculenta leaf extract as bio-reductant, and fabricated with a starch-based HPU by an in situ polymerization process. Structural characterizations were performed by FT-IR, UV–Visible, EDX, TEM, TGA and DSC techniques. Optical studies revealed optical band gap of RCD-ZnO (2.12 eV) was reduced considerably in comparison to bare RCD (3.18 eV) and ZnO (3.3 eV). The RCD-ZnO nanohybrid-reinforced HPU nanocomposite displayed significant enhancement in mechanical properties like strength (1.9 folds), elongation (1.39 folds), scratch hardness (1.53 folds), toughness (3.54 folds) over pristine HPU as well as improved thermal stability (25 °C enhancement). Furthermore, the RCD-ZnO nanohybrid-reinforced HPU nanocomposite demonstrated high efficiency as a heterogeneous recyclable photocatalyst for degradation of aqueous surfactant contaminants, dodecylbenzenesulfonate (96.7% in 110 min) and commercial detergent (94.8% in 150 min) upon exposure to sunlight, following the pseudo-first order kinetics model. Thus, the current work holds good potential for the development of such high performance sustainable nanocomposite as an efficient solar energy-assisted heterogeneous recyclable photocatalyst for large scale applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
Development and design of an inexpensive and competent photocatalyst that functions under visible light still remains a challenge, in the realm of photocatalysis [1]. Presently, one of the most significant goals in environmental remediation is detoxification of industrial contaminants by means of visible light assisted heterogeneous photocatalyst [2]. Over the last decade, TiO2 has been broadly utilized for the degradation of organic pollutants and considered as a standard photocatalytic material [3]. However, TiO2 restricts its practical applications as it can only respond to UV-light (band gap: 3.2 eV) that comprises barely 4% of entire sunlight [3,4]. Thus, the development of an effective, economical photocatalyst for degradation of organic pollutants is important. In this respect, ZnO proves to be of great potential for water purification and toxic gas detection and thus can be employed as photocatalysts [5,6]. Further, nanoparticle(s) (NP) possesses higher mobility, less particle momentum, greater relative surface areas, superior suspension stability in comparison to their bulk equivalents owing to the great contact area between the reactant molecules and NP [7]. However, ZnO has a large band gap which is a major limitation for the achievement of high efficiency in its structural system [8]. Recently, the nanohybrid technique was established to improve the performance of ZnO photocatalyst, semiconductor-carbon based nanohybrid, semiconductor-noble metal nanohybrid etc. [9]. Carbonaceous nanomaterials like reduced graphene oxide (RGO), graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes etc. prospers in preparation of nanohybrids owing to its adsorption capability, great surface area, superior electron mobility and conductivity that benefit the gas sensitive and photocatalytic performances [10,11]. However, the production of GO, carbon nanotube etc. is extremely tedious and possesses its own downsides with respect to protection and ecological problems [12,13]. Therefore, we emphasized on reduced carbon dot (RCD) by using easily available carbon resources. The graphitized aromatic structure of RCD supports the homogeneously dispersed NP and act as a conductive matrix to facilitate superior contact among them [14]. Moreover, during the process of photocatalysis, RCD can efficiently avert aggregation and volume contraction/expansion of NP and thus, maintain their high active surface area [15]. This, in turn can increase the photocatalytic performance of the RCD based material to some extent. Hence, nanohybrid of ZnO NP anchored with electrically conductive graphitized structure resourcefully exploits the combined benefits of carbonaceous RCD and nano-sized ZnO to develop efficient visible light assisted photocatalysts. Although literature cites successful preparation of several graphene-ZnO nanohybrids [16,17], fabrication of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid using bio-based resources is not reported till now. Thus, an eco-friendly and simple strategy for the synthesis of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid as an advanced recyclable photocatalyst is reported for degradation of organic pollutants. Thus, RCD-based nanocomposites with plasmonic and semiconductor NP offered distinctive advantage as a solar light assisted photocatalyst for organic contaminant degradation [14]. Also, ZnO NP not only enhances the photocatalytic activity due to its plasmonic activity but may also impart antimicrobial property [18]. In this context, advanced oxidation process (AOP) achieved widespread interest in waste water treatment as it decomposes harmful substances without the evolution of contaminants. Again, RCD, with its low band gap of (3.1 eV) produces electron-hole pairs (e−/h+) by absorption of photons (UV) that react with water and oxygen molecules to form hydroxyl (
OH) and superoxide active radicals (
O2 –), respectively [14] which subsequently oxidizes contaminants to H2O and CO2. However, to enhance such photocatalytic activity, the band gap of RCD and ZnO further needs to be reduced. This can be done by introduction of conductive electron (e−) channels to separate e−s which result in their delayed recombination or by developing hybrid materials for the expansion of the absorption region from UV to visible and NIR range [19,20]. The conducting RCD surface presents highly mobile photo-generated charge carrier which consecutively slows down the recombination of e−/h+ pairs [14]. Thus, a nanohybrid of RCD with ZnO (RCD-ZnO) may be attempted in order for reduction of the optical band gap of the entire system and thereby enhancing its photocatalytic efficiency. Additionally, the polar surface functional groups in RCD can interact with the organic contaminants and facilitate surface adhering which improves the degradation of matter within a short time [14]. Most importantly, degradation of organic contaminants using sunlight is a daunting challenge, as most are colourless and do not absorb solar light. In this context, surfactants are one of the most persistent classes of organic pollutants, typically discharged with waste water. The bio-elimination and biodegradation of organic surfactants by bacteria are slow and thus, cause ecological pollution [21,22]. Although, biological treatment is a facile route to decontaminate surfactants, problems such as decreased activity of the active sludge, enhanced foam formation and retarded biodegradation of the accompanying pollutants are prevalent [23]. Dodecyl-benzenesulfonate (DBS) is one of the major surfactants present in synthetic detergents and are used worldwide in both domestic and industrial applications, and therefore it is a common polluting agent [24]. In this juncture, photocatalysis can be judiciously employed to enhance the degradation of such organic surfactant based pollutants. The use of semiconductor colloids as photocatalyst for a variety of chemical reactions, due to their peculiar optoelectronic and photocatalytic properties is well known [25]. Photocatalytic e−/h+ redox potentials of size quantized semiconductor nanocrystals can be tuned to achieve increased catalytic effect for selective photochemical reaction. However, as photocatalysis occur at NP, nano-sized semiconductor with high surface to volume ratio will contribute to increase the decomposition rate [26].

Thus, an attempt was made to obtain a novel RCD-ZnO nanohybrid using facile and eco-friendly hydrothermal technique. In order to enhance the photocatalytic efficiency of the nanohybrid, different weight percentages of RCD-ZnO can be incorporated into the starch modified hyperbranched polyurethane (HPU) to help in easy separation and recycling of the photocatalyst [27]. To date, there have been no reports on HPU nanocomposites that feature self-cleaning properties for removal of surfactants from the environment. Both nanocomposites and bare nanohybrid exhibited potential to be employed as photocatalysts for degradation of DBS and a commercial detergent under normal sunlight which is crucial for their further large scale practical application.

2,4/ 2,6-toluene diisocyanate (TDI, Merck, Germany) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2, Sigma Aldrich, Germany) were utilized as received. Poly(ε-caprolactone) diol (PCL, M n = 3000 g/mol Solvay Co., UK), 1, 4-Butanediol (BD, Merck, Germany) and soluble starch (Sigma Aldrich, Germany, Mn = 342.30 g/mol) were used subsequent to drying, overnight, under vacuum, at 50 °C. Xylene (Merck, India) was vacuum-dried and stored with 4A-type molecular sieves, prior to use. Starch modified hyperbranched polyol (HBSP) was prepared using the same method as reported earlier [27]. Dodecylbenzenesulfonate (DBS, Merck, India) and a commercial laundry detergent available from the local market in India were employed for the photocatalytic studies. The aqueous solutions for the experiments were prepared by taking 50 g/L of DBS and the commercial detergent, in two separate flasks. The chief ingredients in the detergent are sodium alkylbenzene sulfonate (anionic surfactant), sodium carbonate (water softener), sodium aluminosilicate (water softener), alcohol ethoxylate (non-ionic surfactant) and sodium perborate monohydrate (mild bleach).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Colocasia esculenta aqueous phytoextract
ce:section.ce:para: Freshly collected Colocasia esculenta leaves (2 g) were washed with water and grinded roughly using a domestic blender, followed by stirring for 30 min in 50 mL of water at 60 °C. Thereafter, the aqueous extract was collected by filtration under ambient condition, and stored at 25 °C.

In a typical process, 5 mL of ethanolic ZnCl2 solution (0.2 M) was drop wise added to 35 mL of ethanolic NaOH solution (0.5 M), followed by 5 mL of aqueous CD dispersion (solid content 54 mg mL−1, dimension 2.2–5.2 nm) under vigorous stirring. The contents were further subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min for complete homogenization of the reactants. Thereafter, 10 mL of C. esculenta aqueous leaf extract with a few drops of FeCl3 (0.01 M) was added to mixture and the mixture was refluxed for 2 h at 80 °C. Subsequently, the solution was cooled to room temperature to obtain a light brown coloured precipitate of RCD-Zn(OH)2. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and washed with deionized water several times to remove the unreacted contents. Ultimately, the RCD-Zn(OH)2 was vaccum dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain RCD-ZnO nanohybrid.

BD (0.36 g, 0.004 mol) and PCL (6 g, 0.002 mol) were taken in a three necked round bottom flask, equipped with a mechanical stirrer and a nitrogen gas inlet. Subsequently, at room temperature, TDI (0.007 mol, 1.22 g) was added slowly into the mixture and stirred at (70 ± 2) °C for 3 h (maintaining NCO/OH ratio = 1). After the completion of the reaction, a viscous mass was obtained as the pre-polymer. Next, in the second step, HBSP (1.4 g, 0.01 mol, branch generating moiety) was added to the pre-polymer along with the remaining amount of TDI (0.35 g, 0.002 mol) (maintaining NCO/OH = 1). Steadily the temperature was raised to (80 ± 2) °C and stirred for 2 h, continuously. Afterwards the reaction was cooled down to room temperature. RCD-ZnO nanohybrid dispersion in THF (0.5, 1 and 2 wt% with respect to HPU) was added to the reaction mixture and the temperature was steadily raised to (80 ± 2) °C. The reaction was run for 2 h until its completion. Similarly, three separate polymerization reactions were performed to obtain HPU/RCD-ZnO (HPU/RZN) nanocomposites with 0.5, 1 and 2 wt% of RCD-ZnO, as HPU/RZN0.5, HPU/RZN1 and HPU/RZN2, respectively. Moreover, HPU without nanohybrid was also prepared for comparison.

Separate organic surfactant solutions containing DBS and a commercial detergent were used for the determination of photocatalytic degradation activity of HPU/RZN. In a typical procedure, small pieces of HPU/RZN2 film (0.5 g) were taken in two separate flasks containing 100 mL aqueous solution of DBS (50 ppm) and a commercial detergent (50 ppm). The solutions were stirred under normal solar light of 60000–80000 lx at room temperature. The changes in concentrations of DBS and the commercial surfactant were monitored by determining UV absorbance intensity at wavelengths of 221 nm under specified time intervals. The activity of the catalyst was calculated from the rate of change of concentration of the surfactant. The same experiment was also conducted using same amount of the nanohybrid for comparison purposes. The amount of the degraded contaminants was obtained by using the following equation.
(1)
Degradation
%
=
C 0
- C
/
C 0
× 100
where C0 = initial concentration and C = concentration after photocatalytic degradation of the pollutant.

UV–visible absorption spectra of nanomaterials were recorded in aqueous solutions by employing UV spectrophotometer, Hitachi (U2001, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffractometer (powder XRD), ‘Miniflex’ (Rigaku Corporation Japan) was used for X-ray diffraction analysis of the purified nanomaterials at a scanning rate 2.0 min−1, over the range of 2θ = (10–90)°. The elemental composition of the nanomaterial was confirmed by using an EDX spectrophotometer (JEOL-JSM 6390, Japan). The dispersion and size of NP within the nanocomposite matrix was determined by employing a JEOL JEMCXII transmission electron microscope (TEM) (operating voltage = 200 kV). Nicolet FTIR spectrophotometer (Impact-410, Madison, USA) was employed to record the FTIR spectra of the nanomaterials and nanocomposites, using KBr pellets. A Universal Testing Machine (UTM, load cell 500 N, crosshead speed 50 mm/min, Zwick Z010, Germany) was used to determine the mechanical properties (elongation at break, tensile strength and toughness) of the nanocomposites (ASTM D 638). A scratch hardness tester, Model No.705 (Sheen instrument limited, UK) equipped with a stylus accessory at a travel speed of (30–40) mm/s was used to determine the scratch hardness of the nanocomposite. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential canning calorimetric (DSC) studies were used for evaluating thermal properties of the nanocomposites. A PerkinElmer 4000 thermal instrument was employed for Thermogravimetric investigation at a scanning rate of 10 °C/min, in the temperature range of (25–700) °C, under inert atmosphere of nitrogen (gas flow rate = 30 mL/min). A PerkinElmer DSC 6000 (USA) instrument was used to conduct the differential scanning calorimetric investigation in the temperature range of (−50 to +200) °C under nitrogen atmosphere (flow rate = 30 mL/min) at a scanning rate of 3 °C/min, following a cycle of heating–cooling-heating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para:
RCD-ZnO nanohybrid was prepared by a facile solution co-precipitation technique involving in situ reduction of CD and Zn precursor (Scheme 1 ). Initially, Zn precursor and CD were mixed together in an alkaline medium, forming a Zn2+-CD complex. As reported in the earlier reports, the surfaces of CDs are supplemented with various polar functional groups like hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxylic groups, etc. [28,29]. These peripheral surface polar groups facilitated the complexation of Zn2+ ions with CD. It is well known that CD can be further converted to their reduced state, improving certain properties along the way [14]. Reduction of CD results in decrease of peripheral oxygeneous surface functional groups and generation of near-graphitic core structure, which augments photocatalytic activity. Also, plant phytochemicals acts as very good bio-reducing agents, owing to their high polyphenol contents [13]. In this route, the reduction of CDs was driven by polyphenols present in the C. esculenta phytoextract. This polyphenolic reduction process was further augmented by presence of Fe3+ ion, forming an effective Fe3+-polyphenol reducing complex [13,14]. Subsequently, Zn2+ ions in presence of alkaline medium formed Zn(OH)2, which combined with simultaneously in situ reduced CDs or RCDs, resulting in the formation of RCD-Zn(OH)2. Ultimately, RCD-Zn(OH)2 underwent dehydration, where Zn(OH)2 phase converts to ZnO, generating RCD-ZnO nanohybrid [30]. The FTIR spectrum of the nanohybrid revealed changes in functional groups of CD pertaining to the formation RCD-ZnO (Fig. 1 a). FTIR bands of the nanohybrid were observed at 1064 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1263 cm−1 (COC stretching), 1735 cm−1 (CO stretching) and a broad band at around 3404 cm−1 (OH stretching) show the presence of carbonyl, carboxylic and hydroxyl groups in CD, respectively. Most of the oxygeneous groups present in CD are diminished or completely absent in the spectrum of RCD-ZnO. There was a decrease in the relative intensity of the broad band for the hydroxyl group (at 3404 cm−1), whereas the sharp band due to epoxy group (918 cm−1) diminished due to the efficient reduction of CD [14,29]. The XRD patterns of the nanohybrid clearly confirmed the presence of ZnO in the system (Fig. 1b). The nanohybrid displayed all the diffraction peaks corresponding to (1 0 0), (0 0 2), (1 0 1), (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (1 0 3) and (1 1 2) Braggs lattices of the ZnO hexagonal structure (JCPDS 79-2205) [31]. The broad peak of RCD appeared at 23.2° corresponding to (0 0 2) plane of graphitic carbons [14]. This suggested that RCD and ZnO are embedded together and held by good interfacial interactions. The Raman spectrum of RCD-ZnO (Fig. 1c) with the characteristic D band (1361 cm−1), G band (1593 cm−1) and 2D band (2914 cm−1) clearly specified an increase in the ID/IG ratio on transformation of CD (1.16) to RCD-ZnO nanohybrid (1.64). This is ascribed to the fact that upon reduction of CD, there is an increase in number of sp2 domains and at the same time decrease in their average sizes. This outcome reflects the elimination of various oxygeneous groups present in RCD, leading to the restoration of aromatic conjugation of their domains, but further generating a disordered structure due to the intensive interactions between ZnO species and RCD [32]. These interactions create new graphitic domains that are smaller in size to the ones present in CD, prior reduction, but more in number which generates a disordered nanohybrid structure that leads to decrease in domain size of the restored aromatic conjugation. The actual content of the Zn, O and C was determined by EDX study at two different points of the RCD-ZnO nanohybrid. From the TEM analysis, the point at ZnO NP is analyzed where the weight percentages of Zn, C and O are found to be 41.4, 22.2 and 36.28, respectively (Fig. 1d). At this point, the C/O ratio is found to be 0.61 which confirms the presence of ZnO. Similarly, the point at RCD is analyzed where the weight percentages of Zn, O and C are found to be 11.8, 5.82 and 82.34, respectively (Fig. 1e). The C/O ratio at this point is 14.1 which confirms the presence of RCD which are in agreement with the previously reported value of RCD. The formation of hexagonal ZnO NP embedded on RCD can be confirmed from HRTEM images of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid (Fig. 2 ). Good interactive forces hold together RCD and ZnO which assist in mutually stabilizing one another. The diameter of RCD and ZnO are found in the range of (3–5) nm and (10–12) nm, respectively. The average nanomaterial size of ZnO has also been calculated from the XRD pattern of RCD-ZnO, using the Scherrer equation, by taking three most intense peaks of ZnO (36.4°, 34.64° and 32°).
(2)
Crystallite
size
Dp = K λ /
B
cos
θ
where, Dp = average nanomaterial size, K = Scherrer constant, λ = X-ray wavelength, B = FWHM (Full Width at Half Maximum) of XRD peak, θ = XRD peak position, one half of 2θ. The average size of the ZnO nanomaterial was calculated to be 11.55 nm which is well agreed with the values (10–12) nm calculated from TEM images.

The crystal lattice fringes of RCD within the RCD-ZnO nanohybrid with a d-spacing of 0.30 nm corresponds to (0 0 2) plane of graphitic RCD as revealed by IFFT image. On the other hand, the distinct lattice fringe of 0.26 nm in the nanohybrid corresponds to (0 0 2) plane of hexagonal ZnO phase. This indicates that the preferential growth of ZnO is along [0 0 1] direction in the nanohybrid [33,34]. Moreover, the SAED pattern of the nanohybrid suggests a crystalline and a well-ordered structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of HPU/RZN nanocomposites

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that carbon based nanomaterials like carbon nanotube, graphene, carbon dot etc. can effectively improve material properties of polymers [37]. In this context, the enhanced mechanical performance of the nanocomposites can be mainly attributed to uniform dispersion and good reinforcing ability of RCDs. RCDs possess a near-graphitic core structure with few peripheral polar functional groups [14]. The incorporation of carbonized nanohybrid into the HPU matrix offered substantial reinforcement of the matrix, by means of physico-chemical interactions [19,28]. This reinforcement provided the right amount of rigidity and flexibility to the HPU matrix resulting in overall high mechanical performance.

Thermal study of HPU/RZN nanocomposites was performed to ascertain the thermal stability of the nanocomposites. The TG thermograms of the nanocomposites (Fig. 4b) revealed high thermal degradation temperatures beyond 300 °C. In fact, all the nanocomposites demonstrated high thermal stability, as compared to the pristine HPU (Table 2 ). This superior thermal stability can be mainly attributed to the reinforcement of the HPU matrix by RCD-ZnO nanohybrid. The RCD-ZnO nanohybrid populated in the free volume of the HPU matrix and interacted with the polymer matrix by various physico-chemical interactions, thereby strengthening the matrix. This reinforcement of the matrix led to the restricted thermal motions of the polymeric chains and imparted resistance to degradation/pyrolysis of the matrix, thus making the process more energy consuming [28]. This thermal stability was further evident from the char residue of the nanocomposites left behind. Incorporation of carbon nanomaterials and metallic particles are known to enhance thermal stability, by absorbing the thermal radiation and hindering the degradation of the polymer matrix [37]. The DTG curves of the nanocomposites (Fig. 4c) divulged a two-step thermal degradation pattern similar to the pristine HPU, accredited to the presence of inherent hard and soft segments in HPU matrix [35]. Notably, some overlapping of the curves was also observed in their corresponding TGA thermograms which can be attributed to a similar decomposition mechanism of segments of the HPU matrix. This observation suggested that the nature and loading of the nanomaterial did not significantly control the degradation mechanism of the nanocomposites [19,35]. Moreover, the nanocomposites displayed loading-dependent enhancement of thermal degradation temperatures, even at very low loading percentages. Enhancement of peak degradation temperatures and char residue percentages of nanocomposites were observed on gradually increasing the loading of the nanohybrid (Table 2). This indicated excellent reinforcing ability of the RCD-ZnO nanohybrid, as more content of nanohybrid incorporated in the HPU matrix resulted in a compact and strong nanocomposite system, less prone to thermal decomposition.

Again, the melting enthalpy (ΔHm), melting temperature (Tm) and crystallinity of the nanocomposite were explored by conducting DSC analyses (Fig. 4d). The Tm of the soft segment shifted to higher temperature with increase in the nanohybrid content (Table 2). The Tm of the nanocomposites improved as the polymeric molecular chains were arranged appropriately due to presence of nanohybrid. ΔHm values can also determine the amount of crystallinity in the nanocomposites (Table 2). The enhancement of crystallinity of semi crystalline polymer with the integration of different nanomaterials has been cited in previous reports. Thus, the uniformly distributed nanohybrid acts as a nucleating agent which arranges the soft segment in a definite direction by boosting the crystallization process [35,38].

The formation of RCD-ZnO nanohybrid was monitored by taking a UV visible spectrum of the initial mixture of CD, C. esculenta leaf extract and ZnCl2, which showed a broad peak at 400 nm corresponding to Zn2+ and a peak at 275 nm attributed to CD [Fig. 5 a]. However, UV visible spectrum of the nanohybrid [Fig. 5b] shows that the peak at 400 nm exhibited a blue shift to 361 nm due to surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of bulk ZnO NP [33]. The spectrum of the nanohybrid also displayed a blue shift of the peak at 275 nm to 268 nm due to the restoration of electronic conjugation on formation of multilayered graphitic RCD structure [Fig. 5b] [14]. Moreover, the UV visible spectrum of nanohybrid exhibited a broad absorption band over a wide range of wavelengths from 200 to 800 nm, indicating effective photo-absorption that would be useful for its photocatalytic activity under visible light [33,34]. The UV visible spectra of HPU/RZN2 and RCD are given in Figs. S1 and S2 of Supporting Information (SI), respectively. The UV visible spectrum of HPU/RZN2 shows a peak at 362 nm due to SPR effect of ZnO NP and a peak at 270 nm due to RCD. The UV visible spectra can also be utilized to determine the optical band gap of RCD-ZnO and RCD from the equation below
(3)
α = C
hv - Ebulk
1 / 2 / h v
where α = absorption coefficient, C = constant, h = plank’s constant, ν = frequency and Ebulk = bulk ‘band gap’. Subsequently, by extrapolating the linear regression at (αhv)1/2 = 0 of the plot hv versus (αhv)1/2, the band gap of the nanohybrid can be calculated. The optical band gap of RCD-ZnO (2.12 eV) (Fig. 5c) was found to be significantly lowered as compared to bare RCD (3.18 eV) (Fig. 5d) and bare Zinc oxide (3.3 eV). This lowering of band gap is due to the overlapping of energy levels of RCD and ZnO, upon formation of nanohybrid. The overlapping of energy levels resulted in increasing of the valence band maximum and decreasing of the conductance band minimum, thereby lowering the overall band gap of the nanohybrid [39]. The result of band gap confirms the in situ formation of a nanohybrid with unique structure. Moreover, the optimum band gap of HPU/RZN2 was determined to be 2.08 eV, which is comparable to that of bare nanohybrid (Fig. S3 of SI). The slight decrease in the band gap of the nanocomposite can be attributed to the physico-chemical interactions between the nanohybrid and the HPU matrix. These physico-chemical interactions enabled further efficient charge carrying ability of the photo-generated electron hole pairs, thus leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity under sunlight. The photoluminescence (PL) study of RCD-ZnO provides insight into its optical activity and transfer behavior of the photo-generated charge carriers. The PL emission spectra of RCD-ZnO (1 mg/mL) revealed a broad maximum emission peak at 698 nm on excitation at 340 nm, which was found to be comparable with the maximum emission peak of RCD (1 mg/mL) at 857 nm under the same excitation wavelength (Fig. 6 a). It was observed that the PL intensity of RCD-ZnO was considerably reduced compared to that of RCD. This reduction in PL intensity suggested delay in recombination of photo-generated charge carriers in the RCD-ZnO heterostructure, resulting from the effective charge transfer between ZnO phase and RCD phase of the nanohybrid [40]. A higher PL intensity designates higher recombination rate of photo-induced e−/h+ pairs and vice-versa. Photocatalysts with lower PL intensity exhibits higher photocatalytic efficiency due to the lower recombination rate of photo-induced e−/h+ pairs [41,42]. Therefore, RCD-ZnO has better photocatalytic efficiency than RCD and hence it is used as a nano-reinforcing agent in fabrication of HPU/RZN nanocomposites. Furthermore, the RCD-ZnO nanohybrid also displayed excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission in the excitation wavelength range of (320–420) nm, reaching florescence maximum at an excitation of 340 nm (Fig. 6b).

A prospective mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of organic surfactants using solar light was proposed (Scheme 3 ). Photocatalysis based on NP involves effective capture of solar light, generation of e−/h+ pairs and efficient use of e−, h+, or both before they are recombined. Nevertheless, for a semiconductor NP, the excitation recombination lifetime is usually on the picoseconds scale [35,43]. As a result, quick excitation recombination leads to ineffective photocatalytic reactions. Herein, RCD-ZnO nanohybrid was mainly selected for two reasons. First reason being, RCD-ZnO was used for the efficient capturing of solar light with reduction in the effective band gap of RCD and ZnO. And second, RCD-ZnO can delay the e−/h+ pair recombination of RCD which allows efficient charge separation and photocatalytic activity. The electrostatic interaction between anionic RCD and cationic ZnO enhances the above factors. The electrostatic force among ZnO and RCD results in tunneling of photo-excited e−s which is employed for photocatalytic degradation of surfactants. Furthermore, the high surface area and polar peripheral functional groups of the nanohybrid makes it accessible to the surfactants. Thus, the nanohybrid was found to be a suitable photocatalyst for the efficient degradation of surfactants and detergents. The surface plasmon of ZnO is excited by solar light which results in the coherent oscillation of e−s, thus producing a high concentration of energetic e−s on their surface. The π-conjugated RCD surface adjacent to ZnO rapidly transports the e−s by its extended π-conjugation structure and leads to the efficient separation of e−/h+ pairs. Basically, the low band gap of nanohybrid results in this phenomenon of e- transfer. The e−s react with dissolved O2 to produce reactive oxidative species (
O2–, O2 2–) whereas the h+s on ZnO might react with H2O molecules to generate
OH radicals. The degradation of the organic contaminants is caused by these reactive oxidative species by an oxidative pathway. The literature cites the photocatalytic degradation of organic molecules into smaller molecules with a few intermediates [44]. Giahi et al. reported the complete degradation of 30 mg/L linear alkyl benzene in presence of 0.2 g/L ZnO under UV irradiation for 0–2 h [5]. Again, Samadi et al. degraded 98% of 10 mg/L anionic surfactant using 150 mg/L ZnO nano powder under UV irradiation for 40 min [6]. Again, Szabó-Bárdos et al. reported complete photocatalytic degradation of 300 mg/L of lauryl benzenesulfonate using 1 g/dm3 TiO2 under UV irradiation for 6 h [45]. Bazkiaei et al. degraded 96.1% linear alkylbenzenesulfunic acid (LABSA, 20 mg/L) using TiO2 NP (5 mg) along with 1 mM K2S2O8 at pH = 6 under 150 min of UV irradiation [46]. Lizama et al. reported 80% degradation of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, 10 mg/L) using TiO2 immobilized on glass (10 g/L) under UV light irradiation for 60 min [47]. Aoudjit et al. degraded 91.4% of SDS using TiO2-layered double hydroxides (10 g/L) under 50 min of UV irradiation [48]. In the current study, it was observed that 0.5 g of HPU/RZN2 nanocomposite film can degrade 96% of DBS (50 mg/L) and 94% (50 mg/L) of detergent water under 110 min and 150 min of solar light irradiation. Table 3 represents a comparison of degradation of different surfactants by various reported photocatalysts with the present photocatalyst. From Table 3, we can conclude that the HPU/RZN2 photocatalyst showed better performance in photocatalytic degradation of surfactants as compared to others semi-conductors previously reported in the literature.

The photocatalytic degradation of surfactants and detergents is shown by the plots of optical absorbance against wavelength at different times for both HPU/RZN2 and RCD-ZnO (Fig. 7 a and b). The effective degradation of the surfactants is exhibited by their concentration changes with time as determined by UV absorbance (Fig. 7c and d). Moreover, to determine the effect of superiority of HPU/RZN2 nanocomposite in comparison to RCD-ZnO nanohybrid, the photocatalytic degradation of the surfactant and detergent was conducted using the nanohybrid. It was observed that 0.01 g of RCD-ZnO required 3.5 h of sunlight irradiation for 95% degradation of DBS. The superiority of the HPU nanocomposite over the nanohybrid is evidently indicated by the results. The polar functional groups of HPU may interact with that of the organic pollutants and adhere on the surface, facilitating good interaction with active oxygen radicals. Therefore, the nanocomposite has improved catalytic effectiveness owing to its good interaction with the nanohybrid and its ability to avoid leaching of the materials [8,28]. The photocatalytic degradation is also performed under dark condition with the nanohybrid. The results indicated that even after 10 h, there was no decrease of intensity as measured by UV–visible spectroscopy. Therefore, the possibility of elimination of the organic pollutants by adsorption of the nanomaterials is eliminated. To demonstrate the self-degradation of the OC, additional experiments were designed without use of nanomaterials under sunlight. The results indicated that 10 h of solar irradiation displayed no decrease in their intensities which ruled out the possibility of self-degradation of the organic contaminants (Fig. 7c and d). In order to illustrate the photodegradation behavior of the nanocomposites, the pseudo-first order kinetic model equation is used. Also previous literature cites the use of pseudo first order reaction to study the photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants [49,50]. Hence we studied using the same model.
(4)
- dC / dt =
K 1
t
where C = concentration of pollutants, t = time and K1 = apparent rate constant. After integrating equation (i), the following equation is obtained:
(5)
ln
C /
C 0
= -
K 1
t
where C0 = initial concentration (at t = 0) of the OCs. The fitting plots of ln(C/C0) versus time display the photocatalytic degradation of surfactants with over 0.94 fitting coefficients, which represents a regular photo-degradation behavior. The fitting coefficients of the photocatalytic degradation of DBS (89% degradation) (Fig. S4 of SI) and commercial detergent (90% degradation) (Figs. S5 and S6 of SI) using RCD-ZnO are determined to be 0.96 in both the cases. It was also observed that 90% and 91% of DBS was degraded using HPU/RZN0.5 (fitting coefficient 0.97) (Figs. S7 and S8 of SI) and HPU/RZN1 (fitting coefficient 0.98) (Figs. S9 and S10 of SI) under sunlight within 210 min and 180 min, respectively. Also, 90% and 92% of the commercial detergent was degraded by HPU/RZN0.5 (fitting coefficient 0.96) (Figs. S11 and S12 of SI) and HPU/RZN1 (fitting coefficient 0.97) within 200 min and 180 min, respectively (Figs. S13 and S14 of SI). On the other hand, HPU/RZN2 degraded 96% of DBS (fitting coefficient 0.94) (Fig. S15 of SI) in 110 min and 94% of commercial detergent in 150 min (fitting coefficient 0.96) (Fig. S16 of SI). It is also pertinent to mention that the nanohybrid also has the potential to degrade other organic contaminants like dye, pesticides, endocrine-disrupting chemicals like bisphenol-A. As the results are already reported previously in case of RCD, the results have not been duplicated [14]. Nevertheless, the nano-level dispersion of nanohybrid catalyst in water makes its recovery difficult. This results in the requirement of a large quantity of solvent and high speed centrifugation 7000–10,000 rpm for the recovery of the nanohybrid catalyst. Alternatively, the nanocomposite film can be effortlessly recovered from the reaction medium and therefore, can be efficiently recycled. The catalyst was investigated for its recyclability under sunlight for DBS degradation. It was observed that there was no reduction in photocatalytic efficiency even after five repeated recycling of the nanocomposite (Fig. 7e). For a heterogeneous recyclable photocatalyst, the mechanical integrity must be retained. Such photocatalysts should have adequate mechanical properties so that they can be recycled repeatedly, without losing their mechanical strength and thereby prevent the leaching of the nanohybrid from the matrix. In this case, HPU/RZN2 is mechanically robust, hence it can be recycled up to five cycles. To further improve the surface area, the nanocomposite film was chopped into very small pieces and dispersed into the organic contaminant solution for effective access of its surface. The products formed at the end of the photocatalytic degradation of the surfactants are assumed to be CO2 and H2O.

A facile one-pot technique was employed to synthesize reduced carbon nanodot-zinc oxide nanohybrid (RCD-ZnO) which primarily resulted in the reduction of its optical band gap. The fabrication of starch modified hyperbranched polyurethane nanocomposite (HPU/RZN) with as-synthesized reduced carbon dot-zinc oxide nanohybrid by in situ polymerization technique demonstrated solar assisted photocatalytic degradation of a surfactant and a commercial detergent in aqueous medium. Moreover, the mechanical and thermal properties of the nanocomposites can be tailored by suitable preference of nanohybrid loading and fabrication process. The nanocomposite verified remarkable effectiveness for the photocatalytic degradation of the surfactant and detergent under sunlight. Therefore, the fabricated nanocomposite can provide environmental remediation as a prospective solar light assisted recyclable photocatalyst for water decontamination.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge SAIC, Tezpur University, Tezpur for various analytical support and SAIF, NEHU, Shillong, India for HRTEM analyses.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.03.248.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
